title: Celadon glaze on carved stoneware
subtitle: Dipping carved bisqueware into a pale green-grey celadon glaze
excerpt: A pre-mixed celadon glaze is applied to bisque-fired carved stoneware by dipping. The glaze settles thicker in carved recesses, producing deeper colour there, while the raised design sits slightly lighter. The effect emphasises carved detail without requiring hand-painted decoration.
sourceNotes: Celadon glaze dipping technique on carved stoneware derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: Ceramic work that has been through a first low-temperature firing to harden it without glaze, leaving the surface porous and ready to accept glaze.
A family of pale green-grey glazes with a translucent, jade-like quality. Celadon glazes emphasise carved and thrown surface detail through differential glaze thickness.
Immersing a bisqueware piece in a bucket of liquid glaze to coat it evenly in a single operation. The standard method for studio glaze application.
A wax emulsion brushed onto areas of bisqueware (typically the foot ring and base) to prevent glaze from adhering there.

content.text: is a translucent glaze that pools in carved recesses and sits thinner on raised areas, creating a depth of colour that emphasises surface detail.
gives the most even coat across the carved surface; brushing tends to fill recesses and leave high spots less well covered. Use a pre-mixed mid-fire celadon glaze for consistent results.

attrs.content: Use pre-mixed commercial glazes only. Do not mix raw glaze materials without a P100 respirator, adequate ventilation, and proper dust-control measures. Wear an N95 mask throughout glaze sessions. Clean up wet — never dry sweep. Dedicate all glaze containers and surfaces to clay use only.

items.name: Pre-mixed celadon glaze (mid-fire)
items.quantity: enough to fill a dipping bucket to cover the piece

items.name: Pottery brush, mop (for touching up tong marks)

content.text: of any dust with a damp sponge and allow it to dry completely. Apply
to the foot ring and the bottom 10 mm of the base to prevent glaze from adhering there and sticking the piece to the kiln shelf during firing. Allow the wax to dry for 30 minutes before dipping.
Stir the glaze bucket thoroughly; celadon settles fast. The consistency should be similar to full cream milk.
Grip the piece with the glaze tongs around the base or foot ring. Lower it into the glaze bucket and submerge fully for 3-4 seconds. Lift out and hold above the bucket for 10 seconds to drain.

Set the piece on the banding wheel and inspect the glaze coat. The surface should have an even, opaque-looking coat approximately 1 mm thick. Carved recesses hold slightly more glaze, which is the desired effect.
Touch up the three tong marks with a small brush dipped in glaze. Allow the piece to dry before loading the kiln.
Apply an iron oxide wash to the bisqueware before dipping. This adds a brown accent in the deepest recesses.
Brush a second thin coat of celadon over the carved areas only. This deepens the contrast.

items.symptom: Glaze coat looks patchy or thin on raised surfaces
items.cause: Glaze too thin in the bucket; insufficient clay content in suspension
items.fix: Stir the glaze more thoroughly before dipping. If the bucket has not been used recently, glaze solids may have settled to a hard layer at the bottom; stir up from the base

items.symptom: Glaze crawls away from carved edges during firing, exposing bare clay
items.cause: Glaze applied too thick; it contracts too much during firing
items.fix: Dip for 2 seconds only and allow the first coat to dry before deciding whether a second dip is needed
